text: The surgeon begins dissection by pulling down on the median umbilical ligament, creating a pneumodissection effect. An initial horizontal incision is made into the peritoneum, avoiding downward trailing. Dissection proceeds medially towards the symphysis pubis using two instruments with the fenestrated bipolar facing upwards to avoid bladder injury. The surgeon then works laterally, using the fenestrated bipolar to grasp the peritoneum against the pelvic wall for blunt dissection, ensuring the peritoneum is pulled inferiorly. The femoral nerve is identified, and care is taken to avoid nerve injury by staying close to the peritoneum.

The surgeon meets the medial and lateral dissections in the middle, revealing the defect area and the inferior epigastric vessels slightly to the left. The dissection progresses inferiorly, exposing the symphysis pubis and extending one to two centimeters below it for mesh placement. The pubic tubercle at the midline serves as a landmark for crossing to the opposite side. Fat is dissected from the femoral canal, with the iliac/femoral vein marking its lateral border.

The surgeon exposes the femoral vein during dissection inferior to the symphysis pubis, revealing the obturator foramen. The lateral border of the indirect defect is checked for a lipoma of the cord, which is absent in this patient.

The surgeon continues the inferior dissection to peel the peritoneum away from the small indirect defect, revealing the chord structures medially and vessels laterally. Using scissors, the surgeon carefully dissects fatty tissue, pulling it medially away from the iliac vein to create space for a twelve by sixteen piece of mesh to lie flat. The edge of the peritoneum is visible, and gentle traction is applied to avoid injury to the iliac vein.

The surgeon identifies an indirect defect, a direct defect, and a femoral defect in the surgical field. The iliac vein and obturator foramen are visible, along with the symphysis pubis across the midline. The surgical area is prepared for the placement of a twelve by sixteen centimeter pro-grit mesh, which is marked to ensure its center aligns with the center of the defects.

The surgeon positions the mesh with a small mark at its center near the inferior epigastric vessels. The mesh is opened from medial to lateral, ensuring it lies flat with the peritoneum dissected away by about a centimeter. The mesh is placed inferior to the symphysis pubis to allow the bladder to secure it upon expansion. The surgeon marks the mesh to indicate folds and uses a two-oh V-lock suture on a GS-XXII needle for suturing, demonstrating a technique to efficiently pass the suture through the peritoneum.

The surgeon ensures the peritoneum is completely closed with sutures to prevent small bowel obstruction. Additional sutures are placed for practice. The needle is carefully withdrawn, with the trocar opening blocked to prevent accidental ejection.
